name: Patricia Alvarez
subject: Quick intro — Robinhood Adjusting + HOA Advocates
body:
Hi Patricia,

I'm Duncan — public adjuster in Wellington, founder of Robinhood Adjusting. Came across HOA Advocates and wanted to reach out before hurricane season picks up.

The reason I'm writing: HOA managers tend to be first to hear about resident property damage, often weeks before the homeowner thinks to file a claim. With the 2026 season starting June 1 and three new carriers entering the market in the last 60 days, the gap between "damage reported" and "claim handled well" matters more than usual.

Would you be open to a quick coffee or 15-minute call sometime in the next couple of weeks? I'm not looking to be added to any vendor list — just curious whether there's a low-effort way for me to be useful to your residents when claims situations come up.

Easy for me to come to your office, or anywhere between Boca and Wellington.

Best,
Duncan

name: Sarah Chen
subject: HB 815 + the new 1802 form — quick conversation?
body:
Hi Sarah,

I'm Duncan with Robinhood Adjusting in Wellington. Wanted to reach out ahead of the July 1 HB 815 effective date and the April-revised wind mitigation form (1802) — both move the roofing pipeline in ways that aren't obvious yet.

The short version: roof inspections become a statutorily-required step before any age-based non-renewal, and coating systems on low-slope roofs are now a recognized alternative to full replacement. For a shop like Quantum, that's a recurring billable line and a real cross-sell into coating work. On the claims side, my work intersects with that whenever a carrier underpays a roof replacement.

Would you have 20 minutes for a coffee or call in the next week or two? Not a vendor pitch — just two operators in the same flow comparing notes on where the volume is moving.

Best,
Duncan

name: David Gutierrez
subject: HB 815 changes the closing-table insurance conversation
body:
Hi David,

I'm Duncan with Robinhood Adjusting — public adjusting practice based in Wellington, working homeowner-side claims across PBC, Broward, and Martin.

Reaching out because HB 815 (effective July 1) and the new wind mitigation form (1802) materially shift what's negotiable at closing on any roof 10+ years old. The "carrier won't insure it" objection moves from a $30K replacement quote to a $500 inspection — that's a different conversation for a buyer.

Would you be open to a quick coffee or call in the next couple of weeks? Curious how Prestige is positioning the change with buyers and whether there's any natural overlap on the claims side, where I see a lot of post-closing surprise on insurance pricing.

Happy to come to you.

Best,
Duncan

name: Carlos Espinoza
subject: Carrier panels + pre-season pipeline — quick intro
body:
Hi Carlos,

I'm Duncan with Robinhood Adjusting in Wellington. Reaching out before June 1 because three new property carriers just landed in Florida (combined ~$850M in fresh capital), and approved-vendor panels on all three are standing up over the next 6-12 months. For a multi-trade shop like Elite, first-mover positioning on those panels has historically meant a disproportionate share of post-storm direct-repair volume.

Curious whether you have 20 minutes for a coffee or call sometime in the next couple of weeks. I'm not pitching a service — just want to compare notes on where the volume is moving this season and where the claims side and the rebuild side can be useful to each other.

Happy to come to your office.

Best,
Duncan

name: Lisa Feldman
subject: Quick intro — property management + post-storm visibility
body:
Hi Lisa,

I'm Duncan with Robinhood Adjusting, public adjusting practice in Wellington. Reaching out ahead of June 1 because property managers tend to have the earliest visibility into property damage across a portfolio — often before the owner has even decided whether to file.

Would you have time for a quick coffee or 15-minute call in the next couple of weeks? Not pitching a vendor relationship — just curious how Coastal handles the gap between damage report and claims handling, and whether there's a low-effort way for me to be useful to your owners when a storm or water situation comes up.

Easy for me to come to wherever's convenient.

Best,
Duncan

name: James Rivera
subject: Pre-season intro — Robinhood Adjusting
body:
Hi James,

I'm Duncan with Robinhood Adjusting in Wellington. Reaching out before June 1 since this is the window where contractors who get on the new carrier vendor panels tend to capture the bulk of post-storm direct-repair volume — three new entrants approved this month, applications open now.

Would you have time for a coffee or quick call in the next couple of weeks? My side is the claims piece — I work homeowner-side getting paid fairly on roof, water, and structural claims. There's natural overlap with anyone doing the rebuild work, and I'd love to compare notes on what you're seeing in Deerfield Beach this season.

Happy to come to you.

Best,
Duncan

name: Robert Martinez
subject: Quick intro before hurricane season
body:
Hi Robert,

I'm Duncan with Robinhood Adjusting — public adjuster in Wellington. Came across Storm Shield's Category 5 system specialization and wanted to reach out before June 1.

The reason: HB 815 effective July 1 and the April-revised wind mitigation form change the roofer pipeline in ways that benefit shops doing the higher-spec installations. Carriers can no longer deny coverage based on roof age alone, and the new 1802 form is what they're actually pricing off — Cat-5 rated systems with current credits documented are going to clear the closing table faster than the general market.

Would you be open to a coffee or quick call in the next couple of weeks? Not a pitch — just two shops in adjacent parts of the same flow comparing notes on where the volume is moving.

Happy to come to your office in Palm Beach.

Best,
Duncan

name: Michael Fitzpatrick
subject: Following up — wanted to reset the conversation
body:
Hi Michael,

Wanted to drop a quick note. I sent a few emails over the last several weeks that didn't quite land — completely my read, those were too pitch-heavy for a first conversation.

Reason I'm circling back now: HB 815 (effective July 1) and the new wind mitigation form change roofer-claims dynamics in ways that actually matter for KLR — roof inspections become a statutorily-required step before any age-based non-renewal, and coating systems on low-slope roofs are now recognized as an alternative to replacement. For a 15+ year shop with your track record, that's a meaningful shift in the inspection-to-replacement funnel.

Would you have 20 minutes for a coffee or call in the next couple of weeks? Less about a partnership ask, more about comparing notes on where the season is going.

Happy to come to you.

Best,
Duncan

name: Michael Thompson
subject: Pre-season intro — claims side meets restoration side
body:
Hi Michael,

I'm Duncan with Robinhood Adjusting in Wellington. Reaching out before June 1 because this is the window where water mitigation firms get the most leverage on the claims side — three new carriers landed in Florida this month, and approved-vendor panels on all three open in the next 6-12 months.

My side is the homeowner-claims piece. The overlap is real: a lot of the water claims I work get underpaid because the carrier doesn't fully credit the mitigation invoice. Would be useful to compare notes on what you're seeing on that front.

Would you have 20 minutes for coffee or a call in the next week or two? Easy for me to come to your office.

Best,
Duncan
